narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and it's clear from your profile that you've been working hard despite challenges. Your application was reviewed by our admissions model, which predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 47% chance of failure—high enough to lead to a rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factors pushing toward this prediction were your LSAT score and your Year 3 school decile ranking, both of which were notably lower than what's typical for students who succeed. Your LSAT, in particular, stood out as the strongest influence pulling the prediction toward failure, well below the average score seen in the dataset. Similarly, your lower Year 3 decile compared to peers signaled potential struggles that weighed heavily against you. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, part-time enrollment status, and Year 1 decile all nudged the prediction toward success, performing better relative to averages in ways that helped balance things out somewhat. Compared to typical students—who often have higher LSATs around 37, GPAs near 3.2, and full-time status—your profile showed strengths in resilience factors like your GPA and background but gaps in those key academic predictors. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law; it's the model highlighting where risks align with past patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your Year 3 performance if possible, and highlighting any extenuating circumstances in a future application. Many students in similar situations improve with targeted prep and reapply successfully—your part-time choice and solid UGPA show you have the grit for it. We're rooting for you to turn this into a comeback story. Keep pushing.
